clues: While controversial whether this disease was carried by Columbus' voyages to the New World, the first well recorded outbreak of this disease in Europe in 1495 caused rapid devastating skin eruptions and flesh to fall from peoples faces, often leading to death within months
Studying this disease, the Tuskegee Study is still a source of medical mistrust, acting as an example of historical trauma
Known as 'The Great Imitator', this disease was treated with mercury for centuries
Introduced in 1988, nearly 30% of hospitalized patients today are prescribed this medication
Available over the counter, this medication irreversibly binds to an ATPase enzyme to block action
While initially considered low risk, long term use of this class of medications has been linked to increased risks of fractures, pneumonia, C. diff, and CKD
Often known as the 'Celtic Curse' due to high prevalence in people of Irish descent, this was first described in 1865
Likely providing an evolutionary advantage against iron deficiency or infection, this disease commonly affects multiple organs
Although known primarily for its endocrine effects, this disorder also can cause arthritis predominantly of the second and third metacarpophalangeal joints
Unlike others in its class, this medication is unique for limited ability to cross the blood-brain barrier due to low lipophilicity causing fewer psychiatric or sleep-related side effects
Due to potential higher mortality rates in the elderly, this medication fell out of favor in the late 90s and early 2000s
Although primarily used for cardiac diseases, this medication can also be used off label for migraines and alcohol withdrawal
Commonly associated with eye findings, some patients may also develop carotenemia, a yellowish discoloration of the palms and soles
Don't be confused, this benign disease can also present with a peau d'orange texture of affected skin, typically over the shins
Beyond causing systemic symptoms, this condition can also stimulate fibroblasts behind the eye causing swelling and scarring, particularly in patients who smoke
